answers.intake_q1: When did your back pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly in your back the pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any recent injury, trauma, or heavy lifting before the pain started?
answers.intake_q4: Any fever, chills, or unexplained weight loss since the pain started?
answers.intake_q5: Any numbness, weakness, or tingling in your legs or feet?
answers.intake_q6: Can you describe the nature of the pain? For example, is it sharp, dull, burning, or something else?
answers.intake_q7: Any pain with breathing, coughing, or movement?
answers.intake_q8: Any history of kidney stones, urinary symptoms, or abdominal pain?
answers.intake_q9: Have you tried any treatments or medications for the back pain, and did they help?
answers.intake_a9: I tried tylenol but it didn't help very much
answers.intake_q10: Any history of cancer, recent infections, or immune system problems?

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of dull aching pain localized to the left side of the lower rib cage area of her back. She denies any recent injury, trauma, or heavy lifting prior to onset. There are no associated symptoms such as fever, chills, unexplained weight loss, numbness, weakness, tingling, pain with breathing or movement, urinary symptoms, or abdominal pain. She has tried acetaminophen (Tylenol) with minimal relief. There is no history of cancer, recent infections, or immune system problems. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: 
The working diagnosis is mechanical back pain localized to the left lower rib cage area, likely musculoskeletal in origin given the absence of red flag symptoms or neurological deficits. 

Differential: 
1. Musculoskeletal strain or sprain 
2. Costovertebral joint dysfunction 
3. Early intercostal muscle strain 
4. Less likely visceral causes such as referred pain from abdominal organs 

P: 
- Medication safety considerations: Avoid NSAIDs if there is a history of renal disease, liver disease, or gastrointestinal bleeding; use lowest effective dose for shortest duration. Acetaminophen should not exceed recommended daily dose to avoid hepatotoxicity.
- Alternatives: Acetaminophen may be continued if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
 - Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen for pain relief and inflammation reduction. (avoid in renal disease; especially severe CKD/dialysis)
- Return precautions: Advise patient to seek urgent care if she develops new symptoms such as fever, unexplained weight loss, worsening or spreading pain, numbness, weakness, bowel or bladder dysfunction, or if pain worsens significantly despite treatment. 
- Testing: No imaging or laboratory testing indicated at this time given absence of red flag symptoms. Reassess if symptoms persist beyond 6 weeks or worsen.

evidence.title: NIAMS: Living With Back Pain (patient-friendly NIH resource)
evidence.snippet: Acute back pain happens suddenly and usually lasts a few days to a few weeks. Back pain that lasts longer than 12 weeks is called chronic back pain.   Who Gets Back Pain? Image Anyone can have back pain. You may be more likely to have back pain because of the following: Age: Back pain is more common as you age. Fitness level: Back pain is more common if…

evidence.title: Back Injuries | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: extending from your neck to your pelvis. Back injuries can result from sports injuries, work around the house or in the garden, or a sudden jolt such as a car accident. evidence.title: AAFP Choosing Wisely: Imaging for Low Back Pain (Red Flags)
evidence.snippet: Information Low back pain is one of the most common reasons for an outpatient visit.
